title: Content Pack Deployment Health (Monitoring and Alerting)
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler app="DA-ITSI-CP-Monitoring-and-Alerting"
| stats count(eval(status="success")) as success count(eval(status="skipped")) as skipped count(eval(status!="success" AND status!="skipped")) as failed by savedsearch_name
| eval health=if(failed>0 OR skipped>success, "degraded", "healthy")
| sort -failed -skipped
description: The Monitoring and Alerting content pack provides pre-built correlation searches and aggregation policies. Tracking deployment health ensures these critical components remain functional.
value: The Monitoring and Alerting content pack provides pre-built correlation searches and aggregation policies. Tracking deployment health ensures these critical components remain functional.
implementation: Install the Monitoring and Alerting content pack via ITSI UI. Enable correlation searches incrementally. Monitor the lookup generator reports (schedule daily). Track notable event flow rates to confirm the pipeline is working. Alert on correlation search failures or skipped executions.
visualization: Table (search name, status, skip rate), Single value (healthy/degraded count).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI, DA-ITSI-CP-Monitoring-and-Alerting.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler`, `itsi_tracked_alerts`, `itsi_grouped_alerts`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Install the Monitoring and Alerting content pack via ITSI UI. Enable correlation searches incrementally. Monitor the lookup generator reports (schedule daily). Track notable event flow rates to confirm the pipeline is working. Alert on correlation search failures or skipped executions.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler app="DA-ITSI-CP-Monitoring-and-Alerting"
| stats count(eval(status="success")) as success count(eval(status="skipped")) as skipped count(eval(status!="success" AND status!="skipped")) as failed by savedsearch_name
| eval health=if(failed>0 OR skipped>success, "degraded", "healthy")
| sort -failed -skipped
```

Understanding this SPL

**Content Pack Deployment Health (Monitoring and Alerting)** ‚Äî The Monitoring and Alerting content pack provides pre-built correlation searches and aggregation policies. Tracking deployment health ensures these critical components remain functional.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler`, `itsi_tracked_alerts`, `itsi_grouped_alerts`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI, DA-ITSI-CP-Monitoring-and-Alerting. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: scheduler. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=scheduler. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by savedsearch_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **health** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Run the search over calm and busy windows. Compare with the Splunk monitoring console and relevant health and introspection views, and spot-check key fields in sample events.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (search name, status, skip rate), Single value (healthy/degraded count).
grandmaExplanation: We track service health, episodes, and related signals in one workspace so the team can see what broke and who owns it without juggling every console.
knownFalsePositives: Episode and score noise from content changes, seasonality, and who is in scope. We check maintenance and drill paths before we escalate.
